item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why is this page not on the End of Death Hub?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I found your scp on the underread and underrated link, I really enjoyed the read and I hope I never find it in underrated again. +1

item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
The first image is by NASA, under Public Domain.

The second image is "The Cloud" by Nathaniel, under CC BY-SA 2.0.

The third image is by StockSnap. Note that as it was uploaded before the Pixabay license change, it is under Public Domain.

The fourth image is "Journey Behind the Falls" by Vassgergely, under CC BY-SA 3.0. It has been slightly edited to crop out the bottom.

The fifth image is by Coconino National Forest, Ariz., under Public Domain.

The sixth image is "Hospital" by Stewart Black, under CC BY-SA 2.0.

The seventh image is "Inside Torrens Island Power Station" by UCL Engineering, under CC BY-SA 2.0.

The eigth image is "Daisies at Ventnor Botanic Garden" by Editor5807, under CC BY 3.0. The colors have been edited.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Absolutely shocking that this never got more attention than it did. It's really a fantastic ending to End of Death, and I was very excited to see this go up when it was a draft. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Votes don't help the author, so I've vowed to always leave a comment with mine, at the very least whenever I rate an article negatively. If you feel the same, help out by leaving critique with more of your votes. Comment follows:

First thing first: the conflict of interest. There's two Maws in this canon, now. There's this one, and there's the one in Tufto's The "Existential Maw Of Despair" National Park!. They might be one and the same to you and they might be one and the same to Tufto but they're not to me. To me, Tufto's Maw is a very specific thing. The Maw in this article is not that. Tufto's Maw is something special to me because it represents the culmination of the aspects of this fictional world that I like the most. It is also a box that can never be opened without breaking it. SCP-5448 is a good, hard look at the inside of that box.

So that's the question this article needs to answer. If I accept this article, then the old Maw is broken and this is its replacement. Does it deserve that? This will form the basis of my opinion.

+ show block
– hide block

Off the bat, the format makes sense to me. The screw is justified. Shotgun approaches frustrate me, but your shotgun of locations is tied together with a continuous thread of exploration. It's a great way to get me to read everything.

Some things about the prose got to me. We're in the deep future. Is the restriction of alcohol to children an inescapable, pure truth about the universe? Are children even common enough to be worth mentioning? There's a few places that could have been used for worldbuilding that instead feel like the article takes place today. In fact off the top of my head, having read the article several hours ago now, I can't think of anything that makes me feel that these events are happening in a completely foreign time.

Some of the dialogue is a bit stilted. It could have used a bit more of a proofread in places. Michaels questioning a McGlocklin who clearly doesn't want to answer is a common thread that I've not seen represented in an SCP before, but then it's turned on its head when she answers with the 'correct' kind of response that Michaels would have expected. I think I wanted to see a character be more assertive about who they are, but it all seems to revolve around Michaels, who's always there to ask the right questions.

The line "Err… I'm rambling aren't I?" particularly rubs me the wrong way. I'll also be forever bothered every time I see "ok" in dialogue in a Foundation report.

As I was reading I was bit confused over why some of these logs were worth including, but then I remembered that the Park is just showcasing whatever they can get theirs hands on. It's an excellent framing device, and it definitely justifies things in-universe, but I don't think I can justify the time wasted on some of these logs.

Forever Falls was too long for me and added nothing to the plot. I'd rather see its character development dispersed more evenly throughout the other logs (which in retrospect is much less 'development' than 'necessary backstory to explain the ending') - I found myself losing interest towards the end of it.

The segment about the magazine people was also too long for what it was worth. I get the point: McGlocklin kept going when the others didn't. But it took too long to get there, and splitting the characters up and having them talking to non-entities means you're losing both plot and character development.

The ending just sort of happens. It's not expected, and it's not a twist. It's just that these events happen to be available to us. The two characters who are arguably the core of the series sort of cameo. They explain, verbally, exactly why they're there and exactly what they're doing.

(there's so much more you could do with this! the new Maw can be exited as well as entered, so there's nothing stopping information from getting out. the halfterlife is real! it physically exists! what does it mean to enter the Maw, then? what does it mean to leave it? why aren't joyce and tony surprised at this revelation? why isn't the Park working to re-secure death? what the fuck is "the event that The Machine activates"? why is the Park open to the public?)

It's also here that I start to lose my suspension of disbelief about the format. Are these really the events that the Foundation would choose to reveal? Is the possibility of death public knowledge? Do Joyce and Tony officially exist?

The imagery from 3448 is used again, and the image of the flowers is cool, but this time I feel like it's there because it needs to be rather than because it means something.

Then the ending happens, and the crux of the article is that McGlocklin isn't suicidal. But I don't see that. Despite all the dialogue in the otherwise-irrelevant scenes that are pointing in this direction, I just don't see it. I don't get the impression that she is different because she's okay with life, but because that's who she is; and I don't get the impression that everyone else is suicidal. Michaels is presented as apathetic initially, sure, but he's more than happy to open up and attempt to chat to McGlocklin. Even when the other three explorers suddenly fall under Joyce and Tony's spell and join them in the imagery, McGlocklin's indifference to it just happens, I don't feel that it's especially meaningful. The constrast is lost on me - it takes until you explicitly state it for me to realise that it's there at all. Even afterwards when I knew the timeline, I went back and reread McGlocklin's interview hoping to find some golden nugget but there was nothing there.

The plot appears in a flash and it's gone just as fast.

I'm really sorry, but I prefer the old Maw. Based on all the above, it's a -1 overall.

However…

I've reconciled the two Maws with my own special headcanon that makes no sense: Tufto's Maw exists, and this document is written by Tsukiko, complete with made-up nonsensical places inside the place she can never visit and analogies taken from her years at the Foundation, all an attempt to stave off the isolation of her eternal isolation by pretending that the only thing that can save her, death, is locked away inside the thing that enslaves her. With that perspective… the things that irk me because they don't make sense suddenly don't need to make sense anymore, and the unexplored plot doesn't need to be explored. Tsukiko can tell herself whatever story she wants.

I don't think this is what you wanted or what you intended, but in this case, two wrongs make a +1.

item.title: Site Crit - Rieview
item.content:encoded:
Oh boy, I'm going to get some responses from Captain Kirby about this.

When I was asked to look this over, I knew one thing about the article. People don't die anymore. I'm a EoD neophyte and that's the angle that I approached this from.

I am both pleased and disappointed to say that this is another banger from CaptainKirby. There's a good sense of place, with well realised characters with motivations that make sense and drive their actions. The exploration is very strong, but for me, never quite passes the line of fantastic, largely because of the thematic reasons I'll get to in a minute. I'm sure this is quite the excellent epilogue to the good work that Kirbs has been putting out for the rest of the EoD canon, but…

+ show block
– hide block

I don't think that the framing device of a park justifies itself thematically or format-ly. For an article that is ultimately supposed to be an exploration of the crushing sense of ennui that comes from eternal life, the concept of this park as a natural consequence of trying to escape aforementioned ennui falls surprisingly flat.

I think this is largely because the actual park is… weirdly mundane? It gets weirder towards the end, but a solid 70% of the article is a fairly straightforward reinterpretation of common ideas of the afterlife - heaven, Valhalla, hell… but there's no real sense of interaction with these ideas or themes that feel, to me, particularly unique.

So rather than having a park that feels like the endstate of a deathless society and the initial exploration being an allegory for those feelings, only for us to know that it's ultimately been commodified into a theme park, we get…

A story that happens to take place in this weird area. And then that area happens to be turned into a park for reasons that feel wholly unrelated to the themes as a whole. And I think there's a version of this that works so much better, and I know Captain Kirby is more than capable of writing it.

I've been accused of being a grouchy purist - I like my format screws to be justified. I don't think this does that, but moreover I think the format screw takes away from the themes in the article, and as such, I'm going to no-vote this one.

These opinions do not reflect any official staff position and are only my thoughts. Please contact Site Crit Team Captain SoullessSingularity with any complaints.

item.title: Happy End Of Death Day!
item.content:encoded:
I've been sitting on this piece for a while, wanted to post this epilogue to Season 2 as a special day commemorating the in-universe beginning of immortality! Hopefully you all enjoy the ride.

I'd like to thank my friend Aria for helping compose and perform the song at the end. I gave her some lyrics for the chorus and she did the rest. You can check out her youtube here (which I'm told will have music posted to it eventually) and her instagram at apmusic.2 (which actually does have music posted to it).

I'd like to thank Stormbreath, Tufto, Oboegeek99, Roget, Riemann for giving me feedback!

(Cited images coming soon)
